title: SCHOOLCHILDREN’S MOVEMENT AND READINESS TO LEARN DURING EXTREME HEAT
authors_full: Allison Poulos, PhD; Jennifer Vanos, PhD; Ariane Middel, PhD; Umar Hassan; Peter Price; Kylie Wilson

summary.hook: This concurrent mixed-methods study in 5 Phoenix Metro (AZ) elementary schools examined recess during extreme heat, focusing on (1) practices/costs of offering recess, and...
summary.summary_30s: This concurrent mixed-methods study in 5 Phoenix Metro (AZ) elementary schools examined recess during extreme heat, focusing on (1) practices/costs of offering recess, and children’s (2) readiness to learn and (3) movement by heat exposure and recess setting.

talktrack_2m.what_this_is_about: This concurrent mixed-methods study in 5 Phoenix Metro (AZ) elementary schools examined recess during extreme heat, focusing on (1) practices/costs of offering recess...
talktrack_2m.what_they_did: Cross sectional observational; concurrent QUAL–quant mixed methods.. Schools N=5; students n=218; staff n=15
talktrack_2m.why_it_matters: Investment priorities: schoolyard shade and dedicated indoor movement spaces (e.g., gyms) may support both learning readiness and physical activity during heat.
talktrack_2m.one_smart_follow_up: How did you define “extreme heat days” for scheduling observations—was it based on air temperature, heat index, or MRT?

key_numbers: Schools N=5; students n=218; staff n=15.
Questionnaires over 6 weeks; recess observations and thermal assessments over 5 weeks.
Air temperatures ranged 91–109°F (33–43°C); mean radiant temperatures ranged 103°F (39°C) under tree shade to 163°F...
Readiness to learn was higher when recess was outside or in gyms compared to classrooms (p<.05).
takeaways: Heat exposure (mean radiant temperature) ranged from 103°F (39°C) under tree shade to 163°F (73°C) on courts.
Aim 1: Essential resources to maintain safe outdoor recess (e.g., shade structures, water bottle filling stations) required years of fundraising.

researcher: How did you define “extreme heat days” for scheduling observations—was it based on air temperature, heat index, or MRT?

How exactly did you measure “readiness to learn” with PANAS-C—what items were included and how were they interpreted as readiness?
